items.title: NTM Ubiquity and Environmental Niches
items.content: Nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) are ubiquitous in soil and water, with specific species occupying recurring environmental niches. For instance, M. simiae is found in certain aquifers, M. fortuitum in pedicure baths, and M. immunogenum in metalworking fluids.
keyPoints: NTM are widespread in the environment, particularly in soil and water.
Specific NTM species have recurring environmental niches: M. simiae (aquifers), M. fortuitum (pedicure baths), M. immunogenum (metalworking fluids).
Understanding these niches helps in identifying potential sources of infection.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: NTM Disease Association with Host Defense Impairment
items.content: Most NTM cause disease rarely unless host defenses are impaired, such as in bronchiectasis or breached by procedures like liposuction. Disseminated NTM disease indicates significant immune dysfunction, while pulmonary disease is associated with epithelial defects.
keyPoints: NTM infections are rare without compromised host defense.
Bronchiectasis and certain medical procedures increase risk of NTM infection.
Disseminated NTM disease suggests significant immune dysfunction; pulmonary disease is linked to epithelial defects.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: NTM Prevalence and Clinical Impact in the U.S.
items.content: In the United States, NTM pulmonary infections are more common than tuberculosis, especially among the elderly. Among cystic fibrosis patients with bronchiectasis, clinical infection rates range from 3 to 15%, increasing with age.
keyPoints: NTM pulmonary infections in the U.S. exceed those of tuberculosis and are rising among the elderly.
Cystic fibrosis patients have a 3-15% rate of NTM clinical infection, higher in older individuals.
Differentiating active disease from commensal colonization is crucial for proper diagnosis.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Role of CD4+ T Lymphocytes and TNF-α in NTM Infections
items.content: CD4+ T lymphocytes are crucial for controlling nontuberculous mycobacterial (NTM) infections, with disseminated MAC disease correlating strongly to low CD4+ counts. TNF-α is essential for granuloma formation and mycobacterial control; its neutralization can lead to severe infections.
keyPoints: CD4+ T lymphocytes are key effector cells against NTM.
Disseminated MAC disease correlates with a decline in CD4+ T lymphocyte numbers.
TNF-α is crucial for granuloma formation and mycobacterial control.
Neutralizing TNF-α (e.g., via infliximab, adalimumab) can lead to severe infections.

items.title: Genetic Susceptibility in NTM Infections
items.content: Specific genetic mutations in the IFN-γ/IL-12 pathway are linked to susceptibility for disseminated NTM infections. Despite identifying several genes, ~70% of non-HIV-associated cases lack a known genetic diagnosis.
keyPoints: Genetic mutations in IFN-γ/IL-12 pathways contribute to NTM susceptibility.
Key genes include IFNG, IFNGR1, IFNGR2, STAT1, and others.
~70% of non-HIV-associated disseminated NTM infections lack a known genetic diagnosis.

items.title: Red Flags for Genetic Investigation in Disseminated NTM Infections
items.content: Disseminated nontuberculous mycobacterial (NTM) infections without an iatrogenic cause should prompt genetic investigation of the IFN-γ/IL-12 pathway. Specific mutations can lead to severe infections, guiding further diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
keyPoints: Recessive mutations in IFNGR1 and IFNGR2 ablate IFN-γ signaling leading to severe NTM infection.
Dominant negative mutations in IFNGR1 cause nontuberculous mycobacterial osteomyelitis by inhibiting but not abolishing normal IFN-γ signaling.
Male patients with disseminated NTM infections, conical/peg/missing teeth, or abnormal hair patterns should be evaluated for defects in the pathway activating NF-κB through NEMO (IKBKG).
Patients with myelodysplasia and mycobacterial disease may have GATA2 deficiency.
High-titer neutralizing autoantibodies to IFN-γ are associated with disseminated infections, particularly in East Asian female patients.

items.title: Characteristics of Pulmonary NTM Disease
items.content: Pulmonary disease is the most common form of nontuberculous mycobacterial infection, often presenting with chronic symptoms and requiring specific diagnostic approaches due to predisposing factors.
keyPoints: NTM lung disease rates exceed those of tuberculosis in North America.
Symptoms include months or years of throat clearing, cough, and fatigue, often leading to multiple physician visits before diagnosis.
Bronchoscopy may be required for diagnosis in patients who cannot produce sputum.
Typical lag between symptom onset and diagnosis is ~5 years in older women.
Predisposing factors include bronchiectasis, pneumoconiosis, COPD, primary ciliary dyskinesia, α1 antitrypsin deficiency, and cystic fibrosis.

items.title: Epidemiology and Presentation of Pulmonary MAC Infection
items.content: Pulmonary MAC infection is prevalent in older nonsmoking white women, often presenting with specific physical characteristics and predisposing factors.
keyPoints: MAC is the most common cause of pulmonary NTM infection in North America.
Infectious onset typically occurs during the sixth or seventh decade of life in women.
Symptoms include intermittent cough and fatigue, sometimes accompanied by chest pain or sputum production.
Known as 'Lady Windermere syndrome,' affecting tall, thin older nonsmoking white women with high rates of scoliosis, mitral valve prolapse, and pectus anomalies.
Nonsmoking females tend to carry several strains of MAC simultaneously, with strain changes over time.

items.title: Mycobacterial Infections Mimicking Other Conditions
items.content: Nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) can present with clinical syndromes resembling other diseases, necessitating careful differential diagnosis. M. kansasii mimics tuberculosis with symptoms like hemoptysis and cavitary lung disease, while NTMs such as M. abscessus are associated with esophageal motility disorders. Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis patients are prone to NTM infections due to impaired macrophage function.
keyPoints: M. kansasii causes symptoms similar to tuberculosis: hemoptysis, chest pain, cavitary lung disease.
Rapidly growing NTMs (e.g., M. abscessus) linked with esophageal motility disorders like achalasia.
Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis patients prone to NTM infections due to autoantibodies inhibiting macrophage function.

items.title: NTM Infections in Skin and Soft Tissue
items.content: Cutaneous NTM infections often require a break in the skin for bacterial entry. M. fortuitum can cause infection post-pedicure if there's prior skin abrasion. Rapidly growing NTMs like M. abscessus, M. fortuitum, and M. chelonae are linked to surgical instrument contamination, causing painful nodules. M. marinum causes 'fish-tank granuloma' from water sources, leading to tendinitis affecting manual dexterity.
keyPoints: Cutaneous NTM infections require skin breaks for bacterial entry.
Pedicure-associated M. fortuitum infection likely with prior skin abrasion.
Rapidly growing NTMs cause painful nodules via surgical instrument contamination.
M. marinum causes 'fish-tank granuloma' and can lead to tendinitis affecting manual dexterity.

items.title: Importance of Including NTM in Differential Diagnosis
items.content: NTM should always be considered in the differential diagnosis for mycobacterial diseases. This is crucial because many laboratories process and culture mycobacteria separately from routine bacteriologic tests, leading to potential underdiagnosis if not specifically requested.
keyPoints: Think of including NTM in the differential diagnosis for any suspected mycobacterial disease.
Mycobacterial sample processing, staining, and culture are conducted separately from routine bacteriologic tests.
Many infections go undiagnosed due to physician failure to request appropriate testing.

items.title: Criteria for Diagnosing Pulmonary NTM Infection
items.content: Pulmonary infection with NTM should be diagnosed based on a combination of clinical, radiographic evidence, and microbiological confirmation. Repeated isolation from sputum or recovery from bronchoscopy/biopsy specimens is necessary.
keyPoints: Diagnosis requires disease demonstrability in appropriate clinical and radiographic settings (e.g., nodules, bronchiectasis, cavities).
Repeated isolation of NTM from expectorated sputum or recovery from bronchoscopy/biopsy specimens is required.

items.title: Distinguishing Between Tuberculosis and NTM Infections
items.content: The PPD test does not effectively differentiate between tuberculosis and some NTM infections, leading to potential cross-reactivity issues. IGRAs are more specific but can still yield false positives with certain NTM species.
keyPoints: PPD test does not well differentiate between M. tuberculosis and some NTM infections.
BCG vaccination can cause cross-reactivity in PPD testing.
IGRAs measure IFN-γ response to specific proteins but may be false-positive with M. marinum, M. kansasii, and M. szulgai.
Large PPD reactions (>15 mm) most commonly signify tuberculosis.

items.title: Radiographic Features in Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections
items.content: Computed tomography (CT) is crucial for identifying characteristic radiographic changes consistent with nontuberculous mycobacterial infections, such as the 'tree-in-bud' pattern. CT can reveal involvement of specific lung lobes and advanced disease features like severe bronchiectasis and cavity formation.
keyPoints: Characteristic 'tree-in-bud' pattern on CT indicates bronchiolar inflammation.
Involvement of lingual and right-middle lobes is common but hard to see on plain film.
Severe bronchiectasis and cavities indicate advanced disease.

items.title: Guidelines for Diagnosing Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections
items.content: Diagnosis of nontuberculous mycobacterial infections, such as MAC, M. abscessus, and M. kansasii, requires specific criteria: growth from two of three sputum samples or a positive bronchoscopic alveolar sample, regardless of smear findings, or a biopsy showing granulomatous inflammation with NTM on culture.
keyPoints: Positive diagnosis needs growth from two of three sputum samples, regardless of smear results.
A positive bronchoscopic alveolar sample can confirm infection without smear positivity.
Biopsy with granulomatous inflammation and NTM on culture confirms infection.

items.title: When to Start Prophylaxis for MAC in HIV Patients
items.content: Prophylaxis against Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) is recommended when the CD4+ T lymphocyte count falls below a specific threshold. This intervention aims to prevent opportunistic infections in immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: Start prophylaxis for MAC disease in HIV-infected patients when CD4+ T lymphocyte count <50/μL.
Effective agents include azithromycin (1200 mg weekly), clarithromycin (1000 mg daily), and rifabutin (300 mg daily).
Macrolide prophylaxis for immunodeficient patients susceptible to NTM is prudent, though not prospectively validated.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria

items.title: Considerations in Treating MAC Infections
items.content: Treatment of MAC infections requires a multidrug approach due to the complexity and potential for antimicrobial resistance. Special considerations are necessary when treating HIV-infected patients.
keyPoints: MAC treatment typically involves a macrolide (clarithromycin or azithromycin), ethambutol, and a rifamycin (rifampin or rifabutin).
Inadequate single-drug therapy can lead to antimicrobial resistance and relapse.
Rifamycin use in HIV-infected patients poses interaction issues with protease inhibitors.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria

items.question: Which mycobacteria are considered nontuberculous?
options: Mycobacterium tuberculosis, M. bovis, and M. leprae
Mycobacterium kansasii, Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC), and Mycobacterium abscessus complex
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, its close relatives, and M. leprae
Mycobacterium marinum, Mycobacterium ulcerans, and Mycobacterium fortuitum
items.explanation: Nontuberculous mycobacteria refer to all species other than Mycobacterium tuberculosis, its close relatives (such as M. bovis), and M. leprae.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: In which environments are NTM commonly found?
options: Soil and water, with specific niches like aquifers and pedicure baths
items.explanation: NTM are ubiquitous in soil and water, with some species having recurring niches such as M. simiae in certain aquifers.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is a major factor for NTM causing disease in humans?
options: Impaired host defense or breached barriers
items.explanation: NTM cause disease in humans only rarely unless there is some aspect of impaired host defense, such as bronchiectasis, or if the barrier is breached.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which NTM species are most commonly associated with pulmonary infections in North America?
options: Mycobacterium kansasii, MAC, and M. abscessus complex
items.explanation: In North America, the bulk of nontuberculous mycobacterial disease in pulmonary infections is due to M. kansasii, MAC, and organisms in the M. abscessus complex.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which cytokine is crucial for mycobacterial control and its neutralization can lead to severe infections?
items.explanation: Neutralization of TNF-α can lead to severe mycobacterial or fungal infections, indicating its crucial role in mycobacterial control.

items.question: Which cells are recognized as key effector cells against NTM at the advent of HIV infection?
items.explanation: At the advent of HIV infection, CD4+ T lymphocytes were recognized as key effector cells against NTM.

items.question: What is the role of IL-12 in the immune response to mycobacteria?
options: It activates T lymphocytes and natural killer cells.
It signals through its receptor via a downstream complex containing NF-κB essential modulator (NEMO).
It is neutralized by TNF-α inhibitors.
items.explanation: IL-12 activates T lymphocytes and natural killer cells, playing a crucial role in the immune response to mycobacteria.

items.question: Which mycobacterial infections are very rare in people with advanced HIV infection?
options: Rapidly growing NTM such as M. abscessus and M. fortuitum
items.explanation: Disseminated infections with rapidly growing NTM (e.g., M. abscessus, M. fortuitum) are very rare in people with advanced HIV infection.

items.question: What is the most critical step in diagnosing nontuberculous mycobacterial infections?
items.explanation: The most critical step in diagnosis is to suspect infection with NTM.

items.question: Which genetic mutations should prompt an investigation when disseminated involvement occurs without an iatrogenic cause?
items.explanation: Disseminated involvement without an underlying iatrogenic cause should prompt a genetic investigation of the IFN-γ/IL-12 pathway.

items.question: What is commonly referred to as 'Lady Windermere syndrome'?
options: Pulmonary disease due to NTM in tall and thin women
Disseminated MAC infections in advanced HIV patients
Genetic mutations leading to severe NTM infection
items.explanation: 'Lady Windermere syndrome' refers to pulmonary disease due to NTM in tall and thin women.

items.question: Which nontuberculous mycobacterial species is most commonly associated with cervical lymphadenopathy in young children in North America?
items.explanation: Isolated cervical lymphadenopathy in young children is most frequently caused by MAC organisms.

items.question: What condition may be mistaken for cancer due to its presentation as isolated intrathoracic nontuberculous mycobacterial infection?
items.explanation: Isolated pediatric intrathoracic nontuberculous mycobacterial infection is usually mistaken for cancer.

items.question: Which organism causes 'fish-tank granuloma' and may lead to tendinitis affecting manual dexterity?
items.explanation: 'Fish-tank granuloma' is typically caused by M. marinum and can progress to tendinitis.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature used to categorize NTM colonies?
items.explanation: NTM colonies are categorized by photochromogenicity, scotochromogenicity, or nonchromogenicity. M. tuberculosis colonies are beige, rough, dry, and flat.

items.question: What is the recommended approach for diagnosing pulmonary infection due to NTM?
options: Isolation of NTM from any clinical specimen
Repeated isolation of NTM from expectorated sputum or recovery from bronchoscopy/biopsy specimens in a compatible clinical and radiographic setting
Presence of intermediate reactions (~10 mm) to PPD
items.explanation: The American Thoracic Society recommends diagnosing pulmonary infection due to NTM only when disease is clearly demonstrable with repeated isolation in a compatible clinical and radiographic setting.

items.question: Which mycobacteria are identified using biochemical, nucleic acid, or cell wall composition techniques?
items.explanation: Current identification schemes for NTM use biochemical, nucleic acid, or cell wall composition techniques.

items.question: Why might IFN-γ-release assays (IGRAs) yield false-positive results?
options: Because M. tuberculosis does not express ESAT6 and CFP10
Due to cross-reactivity with NTM such as M. marinum, M. kansasii, and M. szulgai
Because IGRAs are only specific for latent tuberculosis infections
Due to the presence of anti-IFNγ autoantibodies
items.explanation: IGRAs might yield false-positive results due to cross-reactivity with NTM like M. marinum, M. kansasii, and M. szulgai that also express ESAT6 and CFP10.

items.question: Which pattern on CT scanning is highly consistent with nontuberculous mycobacterial infection?
items.explanation: The "tree-in-bud" pattern is characteristic of nontuberculous mycobacterial infection.

items.question: What is required for a positive diagnosis of MAC, M. abscessus, or M. kansasii according to the American Thoracic Society guidelines?
options: Growth of NTM from one sputum sample
Positive bronchoscopic alveolar sample regardless of smear findings
Pulmonary parenchyma biopsy with no granulomatous inflammation
items.explanation: A positive diagnosis requires the growth of NTM from two sputum samples, a positive bronchoscopic alveolar sample regardless of smear findings, or a pulmonary parenchyma biopsy with granulomatous inflammation.

items.question: Which NTM organism's susceptibility to clarithromycin is indicated for testing?
items.explanation: Testing of MAC organisms for susceptibility to clarithromycin is indicated.

items.question: At what CD4+ T lymphocyte count should prophylaxis for MAC disease be started in patients infected with HIV?
items.explanation: Prophylaxis is initiated when the CD4+ T lymphocyte count falls to <50/μL.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria

items.question: Which of the following medications is NOT mentioned as effective for prophylaxis against MAC disease in HIV-infected patients?
items.explanation: Ethambutol is not mentioned as an effective prophylactic medication for MAC disease in HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria

items.question: What is a potential issue with using rifamycin in the treatment of disseminated nontuberculous mycobacterial disease in HIV-infected patients?
items.explanation: The use of rifamycin poses special problems due to its interactions with protease inhibitors.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria

items.statement: NTM infections are commonly reported to health agencies.
items.explanation: Infections due to NTM are rarely reported to health agencies, making reliable data on incidence and prevalence difficult to obtain.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Human-to-human transmission of NTM occurs frequently.
items.explanation: There are few instances of human-to-human transmission of NTM, which occurs almost exclusively in cystic fibrosis.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Most cases of disseminated nontuberculous mycobacterial infections not associated with HIV have a genetic diagnosis.
items.explanation: ~70% of such cases do not have a genetic diagnosis, implying more susceptibility genes remain to be identified.

items.statement: Bronchiectasis is the best-recognized underlying condition for pulmonary infection with NTM.
items.explanation: The best-recognized underlying condition for pulmonary infection with NTM is bronchiectasis.

items.statement: Pulmonary disease is the most common form of nontuberculous mycobacterial infection in North America.
items.explanation: Lung disease is by far the most common form of nontuberculous mycobacterial infection in North America.

items.statement: Rapidly growing NTM infections are more common than MAC infections in pulmonary disease.
items.explanation: MAC is the most common cause of pulmonary nontuberculous mycobacterial infection in North America.

items.statement: Patients with pulmonary alveolar proteinosis are less prone to nontuberculous mycobacterial infections due to enhanced macrophage function.
items.explanation: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis patients have inhibited alveolar macrophage function, increasing susceptibility to NTM infections.

items.statement: Children with isolated cervical or intrathoracic nontuberculous mycobacterial infection commonly have underlying immune defects.
items.explanation: Children with these infections usually do not have identified immune defects.

items.statement: Isolation of NTM from blood specimens is clear evidence of disease.
items.explanation: Isolation of NTM from blood specimens indicates active infection and is considered clear evidence of disease.

items.statement: The PPD test can reliably differentiate between M. tuberculosis infection and NTM infections.
items.explanation: The PPD test does not differentiate well between M. tuberculosis and some NTM infections due to overlapping immune responses.

items.statement: Isolation of M. gordonae from respiratory samples usually indicates a pathogenic infection.
items.explanation: M. gordonae is often recovered but is almost never a pathogen.

items.statement: MAC isolates that have not been exposed to macrolides are almost always susceptible.
items.explanation: MAC isolates without prior exposure to macrolides typically show susceptibility.

items.statement: Prophylaxis for MAC disease in HIV-infected patients is recommended when the CD4+ T lymphocyte count falls to <100/μL.
items.explanation: Prophylaxis should be started when the CD4+ T lymphocyte count falls to <50/μL.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria

items.statement: Macrolide prophylaxis in immunodeficient patients susceptible to NTM has been prospectively validated.
items.explanation: Macrolide prophylaxis has not been prospectively validated but seems prudent.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria

items.assertion: The incidence and prevalence of pulmonary infection with NTM are increasing among the elderly.
items.reason: Rates of tuberculosis continue to fall, leading to a relative increase in NTM infections.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason is not the direct cause for the assertion but rather an associated trend.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: M. marinum causes cutaneous and tendon infections in coastal regions.
items.reason: It is commonly found in industrial solvents.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as M. marinum is associated with fish tanks or swimming pools, not industrial solvents.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Specific mutations in the IFN-γ/IL-12 synthesis and response pathways contribute to susceptibility to disseminated NTM infection.
items.reason: These pathways are critical for mycobacterial control, as evidenced by identified Mendelian mutations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why specific mutations lead to increased susceptibility.

items.assertion: Patients with disseminated NTM infections and abnormal hair patterns should be evaluated for defects in the NF-κB pathway.
items.reason: These patients may have associated immune globulin defects as well.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.

items.assertion: Patients with myelodysplasia and mycobacterial disease should be investigated for GATA2 deficiency.
items.reason: GATA2 deficiency is associated with increased susceptibility to NTM infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Rapidly growing mycobacteria can proliferate in routine blood culture media.
items.reason: Slow-growing NTM typically do not grow in routine blood culture media, necessitating the use of specific bottles for cultures.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains why it is important to suspect diagnosis and use correct bottles for slow-growing NTM.

items.assertion: The decline in tuberculosis cases has led to a decrease in PPD reactivity.
items.reason: NTM infections are now more likely to account for increasing proportions of PPD reactivity due to the decline in active tuberculosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the decline in tuberculosis cases has led to an increase, not decrease, in PPD reactivity from NTM.

items.assertion: DNA probes are used in many laboratories to identify M. tuberculosis, MAC, M. gordonae, and M. kansasii.
items.reason: Speciation of NTM helps determine the appropriate antimycobacterial therapy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why DNA probes are used.

items.assertion: Inadequate single-drug therapy for NTM is associated with the emergence of antimicrobial resistance and relapse.
items.reason: Treatment of NTM is complex, often poorly tolerated, and potentially toxic.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why inadequate single-drug therapy leads to resistance and relapse.
items.reference: Nontuberculous Mycobacterial Infections · PREVENTION | Nontuberculous Mycobacteria
